threats.title: Router._validateDecimals Underflow Blocks Valid Pool Creation for baseDecimals > quoteDecimals
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateDecimals function performs `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` using uint8 arithmetic. In Solidity 0.8+, this underflows and reverts with a panic when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals. This blocks all pool creation through the Router for common token pairs like WETH(18)/USDC(6) or any pair where base has more decimals than quote. The actual decimal difference might be perfectly acceptable (e.g., 12), but the unidirectional check prevents it entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect that breaks core protocol functionality. Any token pair with base decimals exceeding quote decimals cannot be created through the Router's createPool or createPoolETH functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE — uint8 subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8+ when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals, causing panic revert instead of meaningful check

preconditions: Base token has more decimals than quote token (e.g., 18 vs 6)
User attempts pool creation via Router (not direct Factory.create)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: uint8 subtraction without checking ordering causes underflow for common token pairs
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — this is a pure code bug
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — affects all pool creation through Router for affected pairs

operational_questions: Which deployed token pairs are affected by this restriction?
Can users bypass via Factory.create directly, losing Router safety features?
validation_requirements: Deploy test with base=18 decimals, quote=6 decimals via Router.createPool — expect panic revert
Verify same pair works via Factory.create directly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify any token pair where base decimals > quote decimals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attempt Router.createPool — observe arithmetic underflow revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users forced to use Factory.create directly, losing decimals validation and adjusted liquidity features
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: N/A — this is a DoS/broken functionality issue, not an extraction attack
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — any user creating such a pool is affected

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Router createPool/createPoolETH non-functional for a significant set of valid token pairs
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Pool creation, ETH pool creation through Router

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — any user creating a pool with mismatched decimals encounters this

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed createPool transactions with arithmetic underflow
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users creating pools via Factory.create instead of Router

threats.title: MagicLP correctRState Creates Arbitrage Opportunity After Cumulative mtFee Drift
threats.hypothesis_description: The permissionless correctRState() function resets RState to ONE and sets targets equal to reserves when it detects that the R-state is inconsistent with the reserve/target relationship. Maintainer fee (mtFee) extraction in sellBase/sellQuote systematically reduces reserves without adjusting targets, causing gradual drift. When ABOVE_ONE state has _QUOTE_RESERVE_ < _QUOTE_TARGET_ (or BELOW_ONE with _BASE_RESERVE_ < _BASE_TARGET_) due to accumulated mtFee, any user can call correctRState() to reset the PMM pricing curve, then trade at the discontinuous price to extract value from LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The mtFee is deducted from reserve balances after swap but targets are not adjusted to account for it. Over many swaps, this creates the exact condition correctRState checks for, enabling permissionless state reset and arbitrage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: correctRState is permissionless and resets RState=ONE with targets=reserves; sellBase/sellQuote at lines 230-260 transfer mtFee out of pool without adjusting targets, creating systematic reserve<target drift

preconditions: Pool has accumulated sufficient mtFee extraction through trading
RState is ABOVE_ONE with _QUOTE_RESERVE_ < _QUOTE_TARGET_, or BELOW_ONE with _BASE_RESERVE_ < _BASE_TARGET_
FeeRateModel implementation charges non-zero mtFee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: mtFee transferred out of pool at sellBase:237 and sellQuote:252 without corresponding target adjustment; correctRState at 111-120 resets state when drift accumulates
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After sufficient trading volume with mtFee extraction

operational_questions: How many swaps are needed for drift to trigger correctRState condition?
What is the profit per correctRState reset for typical pool sizes?
Does the FeeRateModelImpl's 50% mtFee rate accelerate drift significantly?
validation_requirements: Simulate repeated swaps and track reserve-target divergence
Calculate arbitrage profit from correctRState reset on a pool with 1000+ swaps
Verify correctRState trigger conditions are reachable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor pool state for correctRState trigger condition (reserve < target with matching RState)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call correctRState() to reset pricing curve to RState=ONE
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute swap exploiting the price discontinuity created by the reset
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single attacker, multi-block strategy using observable on-chain state

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: LP value extraction through pricing curve manipulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be patched without contract upgrade; pattern repeats indefinitely
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All MagicLP pools with non-zero mtFee

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — automated bot can monitor and exploit
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After sufficient trading volume accumulates mtFee drift

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: correctRState calls followed by large swaps in same or next block
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Frequent RState resets on high-volume pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same address calling correctRState then immediately trading

threats.title: MagicLP flashLoan Allows Fee-Free Flash Loans When Both Token Balances Maintained
threats.hypothesis_description: The flashLoan function only validates swap economics when one token balance decreases below reserves. If a borrower returns all borrowed tokens (both base and quote), both post-callback balances remain >= reserves, neither the sell-quote nor sell-base validation branch executes, and _sync() simply confirms the unchanged state. No mtFee or lpFee is charged. This provides unlimited free flash loans that can be used for external price manipulation, governance attacks, or collateral manipulation in other protocols.
threats.investigation_rationale: The control flow at lines 278-282 only reverts when BOTH balances decrease. When neither decreases (tokens returned), neither fee-charging branch at 285-310 executes. The _sync at line 313 just updates reserves to current balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Lines 278-282 only revert if both balances < reserves. Lines 285-310 only execute if one balance < reserve. If both balances >= reserves, no fee logic executes, _sync at 313 completes free flash loan.

preconditions: Pool has non-zero base and quote reserves
Borrower returns at least the borrowed amounts of both tokens within the callback

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Conditional fee logic only triggers on partial repayment; full repayment bypasses all fee branches

operational_questions: What is the maximum borrowable amount from each pool?
What external protocols can be manipulated using these free flash loans?
Does this undermine any protocol revenue assumptions?
validation_requirements: Execute flash loan borrowing both tokens, return exact amounts, verify zero fees charged
Verify _sync state is identical pre and post flash loan

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call flashLoan with large base and quote amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In callback, use borrowed tokens for external manipulation (e.g., oracle price, governance vote)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Return borrowed tokens; transaction succeeds with zero fees

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: External protocol manipulation using MagicLP as free liquidity source; maintainer loses expected fee revenue
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add fee for full-return flash loans

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: FlashLoan events with both baseAmount and quoteAmount > 0 and no corresponding Swap events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Frequent flash loans with no fee revenue generated
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loan usage correlating with price manipulation in external protocols

threats.title: MagicLP sellBase/sellQuote Use tx.origin for Fee Rate Determination Instead of msg.sender
threats.hypothesis_description: Both sellBase and sellQuote pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate determination. If the FeeRateModel implementation applies per-address fee rates, any intermediary contract (router, aggregator, another DeFi protocol) inherits the EOA's fee rate rather than its own. This means fee rates cannot be reliably applied to the actual entity performing the swap. A whitelisted EOA's favorable rates would apply to any contract they interact with.
threats.investigation_rationale: The FeeRateModelImpl currently applies uniform rates, but the architecture supports per-address rates. Using tx.origin means the fee model cannot distinguish between direct and routed swaps, breaking any per-address fee customization.

proof_of_signal.pattern: querySellBase(tx.origin, ...) and querySellQuote(tx.origin, ...) — tx.origin used instead of msg.sender for fee rate lookup

preconditions: FeeRateModel implementation provides differentiated rates per address
EOA with favorable rate executes transaction through an intermediary contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: tx.origin at sellBase:229, sellQuote:246, flashLoan:289 passes EOA address to fee model regardless of actual caller
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires FeeRateModel to have per-address logic (current impl is uniform)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever per-address fee rates are deployed

operational_questions: Does the current or planned FeeRateModel implementation differentiate by address?
Could a future FeeRateModel update introduce per-address rates creating this attack surface?
validation_requirements: Deploy custom FeeRateModel with per-address rates, verify tx.origin propagation
Test swap through intermediary contract to confirm EOA rate applies

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify EOA with favorable fee rate in FeeRateModel
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create malicious contract that performs swaps when called
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Have the privileged EOA call the malicious contract (or embed within legitimate-looking transaction)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Swaps execute at the EOA's favorable rate, not the contract's rate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential revenue loss from fee rate bypass
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires MagicLP upgrade to change tx.origin to msg.sender
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All swap operations on all MagicLP pools

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Swaps from contracts receiving different fee rates than expected
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fee revenue lower than expected for specific pools

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards processExpiredLocks Cannot Process lastLockIndex When Multiple Locks Exist
threats.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks explicitly prevents processing the lock at lastLockIndex when locks.length > 1 (line: `if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex()`). If a user's most recent lock (at lastLockIndex) expires before their other locks, the operator cannot process it until all other locks are handled first. If those other locks haven't expired yet, the user's expired tokens remain locked indefinitely until other locks also expire, potentially delaying unlock by weeks.
threats.investigation_rationale: This forced ordering constraint means expired locks at lastLockIndex can't be freed independently, creating potentially long delays for users whose most recent lock expires first.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex() — prevents processing last lock when other locks exist, regardless of expiry

preconditions: User has multiple locks
Lock at lastLockIndex expires before other locks
Operator attempts to process the expired lastLockIndex lock

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lock ordering constraint at line 397-400 blocks valid expired lock processing
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Operator must follow specific processing order
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When user's most recent lock expires before earlier locks

operational_questions: How often does the lastLockIndex expire before other locks in practice?
What is the maximum delay a user could experience?
Can the swap-and-pop mechanism at line 408-411 cause lastLockIndex to point to an unexpired lock?
validation_requirements: Create user with multiple locks where lastLockIndex expires first
Verify operator cannot process expired lastLockIndex lock
Calculate maximum delay scenario

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User creates multiple locks across different epochs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Due to swap-and-pop in previous processExpiredLocks calls, lastLockIndex may point to a recently-created lock that expires later
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Earlier locks expire but cannot be processed without first handling the (unexpired) lastLockIndex
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User's expired funds remain locked until the lastLockIndex lock also expires
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — occurs naturally through normal usage

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users experience delayed access to expired locked tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires waiting for all locks to expire or contract upgrade
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Lock expiry processing for all users with multiple locks

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: N/A — not an attack, a code defect
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After multiple lock cycles with processExpiredLocks reordering

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users with expired locks that remain unprocessed
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Operator retrying lock processing in different orders

threats.title: MagicLP flashLoan mtFee Extraction Taxes LPs Instead of Borrower
threats.hypothesis_description: In the flashLoan function's swap validation branches, mtFee is transferred to the maintainer from the pool's balance BEFORE _sync() updates reserves. The mtFee comes from existing pool reserves rather than being paid by the borrower. In a standard sellBase/sellQuote, this is fine because the LP fee portion stays in the pool. But in flashLoan, the mtFee is deducted from the pool's token balance, and _sync then records the lower balance as the new reserve. Over many flash loan operations, LP value is systematically eroded as maintainer fees are paid from their reserves.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lines 303-304 and 297-298 transfer mtFee to maintainer before _sync at line 313. The reserves after _sync reflect the mtFee deduction, meaning LPs absorb this cost. Compared to regular swaps where the fee is deducted from the output amount, flash loan mtFees come from pool reserves.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transferBaseOut(_MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.maintainer(), mtFee) at lines 297,303 before _sync() at 313 — mtFee deducted from pool balance, reducing reserves stored by _sync, eroding LP value

preconditions: Pool has active flash loan usage with swap component
FeeRateModel charges non-zero mtFee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: mtFee transferred out before _sync records new reserves, causing target-reserve drift
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Cumulative over many flash loan operations

operational_questions: How much LP value is eroded per flash loan swap?
Does this create a profitable griefing attack (cheap flash loans to drain LP value via mtFee)?
How quickly does the drift trigger correctRState conditions?
validation_requirements: Execute multiple flash loan swaps and track reserve-target divergence
Calculate LP value loss per flash loan relative to normal swap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Execute many flash loan swaps to accumulate mtFee extraction from reserves
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Once correctRState condition is triggered (INV-STATE-012), exploit pricing discontinuity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Combines with OPS-H002 for compounded LP value extraction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Gradual LP value erosion; accelerated correctRState exploitability
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pools with flash loan activity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent FlashLoan events with swap components
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: LP share value declining despite trading fees
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same address executing many small flash loan swaps

threats.title: MagicLP Pool Permanently Bricked When Admin Drains Reserve to Zero via setParameters
threats.hypothesis_description: If setParameters is called with baseOutAmount or quoteOutAmount draining either reserve to zero, _resetTargetAndReserve sets the corresponding reserve and target to 0. With totalSupply > 0 but a zero reserve, buyShares becomes permanently non-functional (neither the totalSupply==0 nor the baseReserve>0&&quoteReserve>0 branch applies). Additionally, any swap involving the zeroed-out token calls PMMPricing with V0=0, which reverts with ErrIsZero. The pool is permanently bricked — no new deposits, no swaps, remaining LPs can only partially exit via sellShares.
threats.investigation_rationale: setParameters at line 422 allows arbitrary baseOutAmount/quoteOutAmount. _resetTargetAndReserve at line 440 sets targets and reserves to current balances. If all of one token is drained, the pool enters an irrecoverable dead state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setParameters allows arbitrary baseOutAmount/quoteOutAmount withdrawal; _resetTargetAndReserve sets reserves/targets to post-withdrawal balance which can be 0; Math._SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade reverts with ErrIsZero when V0=0

preconditions: Implementation owner calls setParameters
baseOutAmount or quoteOutAmount equals the full balance of that token
minBaseReserve or minQuoteReserve set to 0 allowing the drain

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No guard in setParameters preventing draining a reserve to zero; _resetTargetAndReserve doesn't validate non-zero balances

operational_questions: Does the current admin multisig have safeguards against draining a reserve to zero?
Can this happen accidentally through miscalculated parameters?
Is there any recovery path (ratioSync, direct token deposit + sync)?
validation_requirements: Call setParameters draining base to 0, verify buyShares reverts
Verify sellBase/sellQuote revert with ErrIsZero
Test if ratioSync can recover (likely fails due to division by zero)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or malicious admin calls setParameters with baseOutAmount = full base balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All buyShares, sellBase, sellQuote calls revert — pool is permanently non-functional
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: LP holders can only recover partial funds via sellShares (quote tokens only)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Pool permanently bricked with potential loss of LP funds
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete pool failure — no swaps, no new deposits
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irrecoverable without new pool deployment; stuck LP tokens

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin transferring pool reserves to external addresses

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards Reward Claim Permanently Blocked If Any Reward Token Becomes Non-Transferable
threats.hypothesis_description: The _getRewards function iterates through all rewardTokens and transfers accumulated rewards. If any reward token becomes non-transferable (e.g., paused, blacklisted, or self-destructed), the safeTransfer call reverts, blocking the entire reward claim for ALL reward tokens. Since there is no function to remove reward tokens from the rewardTokens array, and _getRewards doesn't have a mechanism to skip individual failed transfers, users permanently lose access to all accumulated rewards including those from functioning tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: addReward only pushes to rewardTokens array (no removal function). _getRewards at lines iterates all tokens with safeTransfer. A single reverting transfer blocks the entire function. No try-catch or skip mechanism exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rewardToken.safeTransfer(user, amount) at line 479 — single reverting token transfer blocks entire _getRewards loop; no removeReward function exists; rewardTokens only grows via addReward push

preconditions: At least one reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, etc.)
Users have accumulated non-zero reward amounts for the problematic token
Reward lock is expired (expired=true in _getRewards)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No removeReward function; rewardTokens array only grows; single safeTransfer failure blocks entire claim loop
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner added the reward token; no way to remove it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When any added reward token becomes non-transferable

operational_questions: Which reward tokens are currently added? Can any be centrally paused?
Is there an emergency withdrawal path that bypasses reward claiming?
Could the owner recover stuck rewards via the recover function?
validation_requirements: Add a pausable reward token, accumulate rewards, pause it, verify getRewards reverts
Verify withdraw() works independently (it does — only withdrawWithRewards is affected)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Reward token added by owner becomes paused/blacklisted by its issuer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users call getRewards() — safeTransfer reverts for paused token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All reward claiming permanently blocked; withdrawWithRewards also blocked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users can still withdraw() staking tokens but lose all accumulated rewards across ALL tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires external token pausability — not attacker-controlled

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All reward claims permanently blocked for affected users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add removeReward or try-catch

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed getRewards transactions reverting on specific token transfers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to claim any rewards despite earned amounts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users calling withdraw() instead of withdrawWithRewards()

threats.title: MagicLP sellShares divCeil Target Erosion Causes Cumulative Pricing Dysfunction
threats.hypothesis_description: sellShares reduces _BASE_TARGET_ and _QUOTE_TARGET_ using divCeil (rounding up the subtraction amount) while distributing tokens using floor division (rounding down). Over many sellShares operations, targets shrink faster than proportional, creating a cumulative discrepancy. Eventually, targets can approach or reach zero while non-trivial shares and reserves remain, breaking PMM pricing (division by zero in pricing calculations).
threats.investigation_rationale: Line 364: `_BASE_TARGET_ = uint112(uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) - (uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * shareAmount).divCeil(totalShares))` rounds up the subtraction. Line 361: `baseAmount = (baseBalance * shareAmount) / totalShares` rounds down the payout. The asymmetry compounds over many operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: baseAmount uses floor division at 361; _BASE_TARGET_ uses divCeil at 364 — asymmetric rounding causes targets to shrink faster than reserves over many operations

preconditions: Pool has many sellShares operations over time
Small share amounts amplify rounding effect

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Asymmetric rounding between payout (floor) and target reduction (ceil) in sellShares
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — occurs naturally through user withdrawals

operational_questions: How many sellShares operations before targets reach problematic values?
Do small partial withdrawals accelerate the erosion?
Can this be exploited by deliberately making many small withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Simulate 1000+ small sellShares operations and track target-reserve ratio
Determine if targets can reach zero while meaningful reserves remain

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Execute many small sellShares to maximize divCeil rounding effect
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Targets erode below reserves, distorting PMM pricing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Trade at distorted prices to extract value, or trigger correctRState for arbitrage

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: PMM pricing dysfunction; potential pool bricking if targets reach 0
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin can call ratioSync or setParameters to reset targets
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Pool pricing accuracy for all traders

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — many small transactions from single address

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Many small sellShares from same address; decreasing target-to-reserve ratios
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Targets approaching zero while reserves remain positive
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Suspicious patterns of minimal share withdrawals

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards notifyRewardAmount Near Epoch Boundary Permanently Strands Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: notifyRewardAmount calculates _remainingRewardTime = nextEpoch - block.timestamp. If called with 1 second remaining, rewardRate = amount/1. If totalSupply is 0 during that 1-second window, rewardPerToken doesn't increase (division by totalSupply=0 returns stored value), and the full reward amount is permanently unclaimable. The tokens remain in the contract but can never be distributed. Setting minRemainingTime=0 bypasses the safety check.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line 406-407: `amount += _remainingRewardTime * reward.rewardRate` adds remaining from current period. Line 413: `reward.rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime` with small denominator creates extreme rate. Line in _rewardPerToken: `if (totalSupply_ == 0) return stored` means zero-supply period loses all rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime at line 413; if _remainingRewardTime=1 and totalSupply=0 during that second, all rewards lost; minRemainingTime=0 at line 403-405 bypasses safety

preconditions: Operator calls notifyRewardAmount with minRemainingTime=0
block.timestamp is very close to epoch boundary (1-2 seconds remaining)
totalSupply is 0 during the remaining seconds

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: rewardRate inflated when _remainingRewardTime is tiny; rewards lost if totalSupply=0
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Last seconds of each epoch with no stakers

operational_questions: How likely is totalSupply=0 during the last second of an epoch?
Does the Gelato automation account for epoch boundary timing?
What amount of rewards could be stranded per occurrence?
validation_requirements: Simulate notifyRewardAmount with 1 second remaining and totalSupply=0
Verify rewards are permanently unclaimable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for epoch where all stakers have withdrawn (totalSupply=0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Operator notifies reward just before epoch boundary
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Rewards distributed over 1 second with no stakers to receive them
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Reward tokens permanently stranded in contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires specific timing of operator action

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens recoverable by owner via recover() if reward token is not staking token

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Near epoch boundaries with zero supply

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reward tokens in contract exceeding expected distribution

threats.title: MagicLP setParameters Allows Implementation Owner to Drain All Pool Reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: setParameters allows the implementation owner to specify arbitrary baseOutAmount and quoteOutAmount, which are transferred to an arbitrary assetTo address before resetting reserves. Combined with the minBaseReserve/minQuoteReserve check being against pre-transfer reserves, the owner can drain the entire pool's base and quote token balances in a single transaction, stealing all LP funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line 422-434: `_transferBaseOut(assetTo, baseOutAmount)` and `_transferQuoteOut(assetTo, quoteOutAmount)` transfer arbitrary amounts. Lines 416-418 check min reserves against pre-transfer _BASE_RESERVE_/_QUOTE_RESERVE_, not post-transfer balances.
threats.config_evidence: MIMSwap.s.sol shows ownership transferred to safe.ops multisig, which provides some protection

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyImplementationOwner can set arbitrary baseOutAmount/quoteOutAmount transferred to arbitrary assetTo; minBaseReserve check at 416 is against pre-transfer reserves, not post-transfer

preconditions: Attacker has implementation owner privileges (key compromise or malicious admin)
Pool has non-zero reserves

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setParameters combines parameter changes with arbitrary token extraction in single transaction
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time admin key is available

operational_questions: Is the implementation owner a multisig with adequate threshold?
Are there monitoring alerts for setParameters calls?
Does the minReserve check provide meaningful protection?
validation_requirements: Verify setParameters with baseOutAmount = full reserve succeeds
Verify minBaseReserve=0 allows full drain

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setParameters with baseOutAmount=full base balance, quoteOutAmount=full quote balance, minBaseReserve=0, minQuoteReserve=0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All pool tokens transferred to attacker's assetTo address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pool is permanently bricked with zero reserves

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete loss of all pool assets across all clones sharing same implementation
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All pools controlled by same implementation owner vulnerable
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pool operations for affected pools

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards recover() Can Drain Reward Tokens Earmarked for Stakers
threats.hypothesis_description: The recover() function only protects the staking token against over-skimming (`stakingToken.balanceOf(this) - stakingTokenBalance`). For ALL other tokens including reward tokens, there is no balance check — the owner can transfer the entire balance of any reward token out of the contract, even if those tokens are earmarked for pending and future user reward claims. This leaves the contract insolvent for reward payouts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line 324-327: The skimming protection only applies when `tokenAddress == stakingToken`. For any other token (including active reward tokens), `tokenAddress.safeTransfer(owner, tokenAmount)` executes without any balance safety check.
threats.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows ownership transferred to safe.ops multisig

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (tokenAddress == stakingToken) check at 324 only protects staking token; all other tokens including active reward tokens can be fully drained by owner

preconditions: Owner calls recover() with a reward token address
Reward token has accumulated balance for user claims

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Insufficient balance check in recover() for non-staking tokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time reward tokens are in the contract

operational_questions: Is there a separate accounting mechanism preventing accidental reward token drainage?
Would stakers notice before claiming if rewards are drained?
Can the owner restore drained rewards by re-depositing?
validation_requirements: Call recover() for an active reward token with full balance
Verify getRewards reverts for users due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised or malicious owner identifies reward token with large accumulated balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All reward tokens transferred to owner
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' getRewards calls fail due to insufficient token balance

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All accumulated rewards for affected token drained
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reward claims fail; user trust destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can re-deposit if honest; otherwise requires intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reward claims for drained token

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time with non-zero reward balance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: LogRecovered events for reward token addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reward token balance dropping below expected pending rewards
